summary: Gruber responds to Marc Andreessen's argument that mobile web apps will inevitably dominate native apps, just as web dominated desktop software. He separates two conflated debates — desktop vs. mobile computing, and native vs. web apps — arguing they are distinct issues. While acknowledging web apps' strengths in reach and app stores' developer friction, he contends that native apps consistently deliver superior user experiences, particularly on mobile. He notes that even the best mobile web apps in 2013 don't match the quality of the first native iPhone apps from 2007, and that Facebook's approach of using native apps as interfaces to web services gets the balance right.
key_points: Andreessen conflates two separate debates: desktop vs. mobile computing, and native apps vs. web apps
App stores are mostly a pain for developers, not users — and the gatekeeper role provides trust and security benefits
iOS proved that user experience matters in the mass market, and native apps still win on experience quality
The best mobile web apps in 2013 still don't approach the quality of the first native iPhone apps from 2007
Web apps maximize reach with least effort; native apps maximize the quality of the experience
money_quotes: The growth in desktop computers is over, and it's never coming back.
App stores are mostly only a pain in the ass for developers, not users.
The same gatekeeper annoying developers with an approval process is the trusted source telling users, This app is OK, no harm will come to you from installing it.
iOS has proven that user experience matters, not merely among a picky few but in the mass market.
What's great about the web is ubiquitous network availability, not running within a browser tab.
Websites are just services, and what you see in a browser tab is merely one possible interface to that service.
Web apps are the best way to reach the most possible people with the least effort; native apps are the best way to create the best possible experience.
Most of the ones I use, I do so because they have no native app counterpart, and I wish they did, so that I could use the native app instead.
key_insight: The web's strength is universal reach, not running in a browser tab — native apps remain the superior interface when user experience is the priority.
